html:
AutoStore is not the absurd 1.5x EBITDA bargain implied by stale data. At about NOK 12.4, the real equity value is roughly NOK 42 billion, with enterprise value around NOK 44 billion. The thesis is better than that: this is a high-gross-margin warehouse robotics leader whose order cycle has turned before the market has fully reclassified it from post-Covid hardware casualty to AI-enabled fulfillment platform.

The inflection is real. Q1 2026 revenue was $165.8 million, order intake was $179.4 million, backlog rose to $570.6 million, gross margin was 72.7%, and adjusted EBITDA margin was 44.0% (Q1 report). That follows a weak 2025 in which revenue fell 10.4% to $538.6 million but adjusted EBITDA still held at $228.1 million, a 42.3% margin. A business that can take that revenue hit and still earn 40% plus EBITDA margins is not a normal equipment vendor.

The market still sees patent expiry, cyclical warehouse capex, SoftBank/THL overhang, and a stock already up sharply from the lows. The variant view is that AutoStore’s installed base and partner ecosystem are becoming more valuable as software becomes the control layer. The March 2026 CubeVerse and AutoStore Intelligence launch matters because it reframes the grid from hardware into a data platform: higher throughput, simulation, analytics, issue prediction, and robotic picking workflows inside existing customer systems (product launch). If AutoStore can price even part of that as recurring software, the multiple should not be set by legacy capex machinery.

The valuation is not optically cheap on trailing numbers. StockAnalysis shows NOK 42.6 billion market cap, NOK 44.2 billion EV, forward P/E around 25.6x, and EV/EBITDA around 20.9x on its generic trailing EBITDA definition (market data). But on company-adjusted LTM EBITDA of about $280 million after Q1, EV/adjusted EBITDA is closer to 15x. If 2026 revenue lands around $680 to $720 million with 43% to 45% EBITDA margins, current valuation compresses into the mid-teens. If 2027 revenue reaches $800 million plus with software attach improving mix, the stock is closer to 11x to 12x forward EBITDA. That is where the torque sits.

The reason to own it is not “net cash” or liquidation value. AutoStore has modest net debt, not excess cash. The reason is that order momentum, backlog, and AI/software attach can make today’s forward earnings power materially higher than trailing screens suggest. The business has more than 1,950 systems across over 65 countries and over 1,300 customers, which creates a dense installed base for upgrades, service, analytics, and optimization. The customer pain is also persistent: labor scarcity, warehouse density, e-commerce service levels, supply-chain resilience, and 3PL throughput.

Retail context is favorable because the stock is not yet a crowded global AI robotics story. Discussion is still mostly Nordic/private-investor chatter and automated signal posts, not broad retail euphoria. Analysts have lifted targets, but consensus is still in the low-to-mid teens, which leaves room for positive revisions if Q2 and Q3 confirm Q1 was not just easy comps and backlog conversion.

The main risk is that this remains a great product but an average stock because growth settles at mid-single digits while valuation already assumes recovery. Patent expiry and competitors like Exotec, Geek+, Hai Robotics, and shuttle/AMR systems can pressure new-system pricing. Tariffs and aluminum costs can hurt, although AutoStore says direct U.S. tariff impact should be moderate because North America was about 24% of 2025 revenue and sales run through partners. The other risk is that CubeVerse is margin defense rather than monetizable software. Watch order intake, backlog conversion, software attach, and cash conversion after capitalized development.

Bear target: NOK 7.5, about 39% downside from NOK 12.4. This assumes Q1 was a backlog catch-up, order intake rolls over, EBITDA falls back toward $210 million, and the market values AutoStore as a challenged hardware vendor at roughly 11x EBITDA.

Base target: NOK 18.5, about 49% upside. This assumes 2026 to 2027 revenue reaccelerates toward $750 million to $820 million, EBITDA reaches $340 million to $370 million, and the stock gets a 16x to 17x EBITDA multiple as confidence returns.

Bull target: NOK 30, about 142% upside. This requires CubeVerse and AutoStore Intelligence to prove they are real software economics, not just product marketing, with revenue approaching $900 million, EBITDA above $430 million, and the market valuing AutoStore as a scarce 70% gross-margin robotics platform at 22x EBITDA.
